chapter_info.subject: Mathematics
chapter_info.chapter_name: A Tale of Three Intersecting Lines

flashcards.q: What is a triangle?
flashcards.a: A polygon with three sides, three vertices, and three angles.

flashcards.q: What are the vertices of a triangle?
flashcards.a: The three corner points where the sides of the triangle meet.

flashcards.q: What are sides of a triangle?
flashcards.a: The three line segments connecting the three vertices.

flashcards.q: What notation is used to represent triangle ABC?
flashcards.a: △ABC, where A, B, and C are the three vertices.

flashcards.q: Define an equilateral triangle.
flashcards.a: A triangle with all three sides of equal length.

flashcards.q: What are the angles in an equilateral triangle?
flashcards.a: All three angles are equal to 60°.

flashcards.q: Define an isosceles triangle.
flashcards.a: A triangle with two sides of equal length.

flashcards.q: What property do the angles opposite equal sides have in an isosceles triangle?
flashcards.a: They are equal to each other.

flashcards.q: Define a scalene triangle.
flashcards.a: A triangle with all three sides of different lengths.

flashcards.q: What is unique about the angles in a scalene triangle?
flashcards.a: All three angles have different measures.

flashcards.q: State the triangle inequality property.
flashcards.a: The sum of any two sides of a triangle must be greater than the third side.

flashcards.q: What is the condition for three lengths a, b, c to form a triangle?
flashcards.a: a + b > c, b + c > a, and c + a > b must all be true.

flashcards.q: Can sides 1, 2, and 3 form a triangle?
flashcards.a: No, because 1 + 2 = 3, which is not greater than 3.

flashcards.q: Can sides 3, 4, and 5 form a triangle?
What is the angle sum property of triangles?
flashcards.a: The sum of all three interior angles of any triangle equals 180°.

flashcards.q: If two angles of a triangle are 60° and 70°, what is the third?
Define an altitude of a triangle.
flashcards.a: A perpendicular line segment from a vertex to the opposite side (or its extension).

flashcards.q: How many altitudes does a triangle have?
flashcards.a: Three altitudes, one from each vertex.

flashcards.q: What tool is used to construct an altitude?
flashcards.a: A set square is used to draw a perpendicular from a vertex to the opposite side.

flashcards.q: Where do all three altitudes of a triangle meet?
flashcards.a: At a single point called the orthocenter.

flashcards.q: Define a right-angled triangle.
flashcards.a: A triangle with one angle equal to 90°.

flashcards.q: What is the side opposite the right angle called in a right triangle?
flashcards.a: The hypotenuse.

flashcards.q: Define an acute-angled triangle.
flashcards.a: A triangle with all three angles less than 90°.

flashcards.q: Define an obtuse-angled triangle.
flashcards.a: A triangle with one angle greater than 90° and less than 180°.

flashcards.q: Can a triangle have more than one right angle?
flashcards.a: No, because two right angles alone would sum to 180°, leaving no room for the third angle.

flashcards.q: Can a triangle have more than one obtuse angle?
flashcards.a: No, because two obtuse angles would sum to more than 180°.

flashcards.q: What is the SSS construction method?
flashcards.a: Constructing a triangle when all three sides are given.

flashcards.q: Describe the SSS construction method.
flashcards.a: Draw the longest side as base, then use compass to draw circles from endpoints with radii equal to the other two sides. Their intersection is the third vertex.

flashcards.q: What is the SAS construction method?
flashcards.a: Constructing a triangle when two sides and the included angle are given.

flashcards.q: Describe the SAS construction method.
flashcards.a: Draw the first side, construct the given angle at one endpoint, draw the second side along the angle, then connect endpoints to form the triangle.

flashcards.q: What is the ASA construction method?
flashcards.a: Constructing a triangle when two angles and the included side are given.

flashcards.q: Describe the ASA construction method.
flashcards.a: Draw the given side, construct the two given angles at its endpoints, extend the angle lines until they meet at the third vertex.

flashcards.q: What does 'included angle' mean?
flashcards.a: The angle between two given sides of a triangle.

flashcards.q: What does 'included side' mean?
flashcards.a: The side between two given angles of a triangle.

flashcards.q: Can a triangle be constructed with two sides and a non-included angle?
flashcards.a: Not always; this may result in ambiguous cases with 0, 1, or 2 possible triangles.

flashcards.q: For ASA construction, what is the condition on the sum of the two angles?
flashcards.a: The sum must be less than 180°.

flashcards.q: If two angles in ASA construction sum to 180° or more, what happens?
flashcards.a: No triangle can be formed, as the sides would be parallel.

flashcards.q: What construction tool is essential for SSS method?
flashcards.a: A compass, to draw circles of specific radii from given points.

flashcards.q: What is an exterior angle of a triangle?
flashcards.a: An angle formed outside the triangle when one side is extended beyond a vertex.

flashcards.q: State the exterior angle theorem.
flashcards.a: An exterior angle of a triangle equals the sum of the two non-adjacent (remote) interior angles.

flashcards.q: In triangle ABC, if ∠A = 50° and ∠B = 60°, what is the exterior angle at C?
flashcards.a: 110°, because the exterior angle equals ∠A + ∠B = 50° + 60° = 110°.

flashcards.q: How is the angle sum property proved using parallel lines?
flashcards.a: Draw a line through one vertex parallel to the opposite side; use alternate angles to show the three angles form a straight line (180°).

flashcards.q: What are alternate angles?
flashcards.a: Angles on opposite sides of a transversal cutting two parallel lines, at different intersection points, that are equal.

flashcards.q: In an equilateral triangle, what is the measure of each altitude angle with the base?
flashcards.a: 90°, as an altitude is perpendicular to the base.

flashcards.q: Can an equilateral triangle also be a right-angled triangle?
flashcards.a: No, because all angles in an equilateral triangle are 60°, not 90°.

flashcards.q: Can an equilateral triangle be acute-angled?
flashcards.a: Yes, because all angles (60° each) are less than 90°.

flashcards.q: In an isosceles triangle, what is true about the angles?
flashcards.a: Two angles (opposite the equal sides) are equal; the third angle is different.

flashcards.q: Can an isosceles triangle be right-angled?
flashcards.a: Yes, with angles 90°, 45°, and 45°.

flashcards.q: What is a 45-45-90 triangle?
flashcards.a: An isosceles right-angled triangle with two equal legs and a 90° angle between them.

flashcards.q: For three lengths to form a triangle, the sum of any two must exceed the third. Why?
flashcards.a: Because the straight-line distance (shortest path) between two points cannot exceed the path via a third point.

flashcards.q: If the longest side equals the sum of the other two sides, what is formed?
flashcards.a: A degenerate triangle (a straight line), not a proper triangle.

flashcards.q: What tool is used in SAS construction to mark angles?
flashcards.a: A protractor, to measure and mark the given angle.

flashcards.q: In ASA construction, after drawing the two angles, what do we do next?
flashcards.a: Extend the angle lines (rays) until they intersect; the intersection point is the third vertex.

flashcards.q: What is the range of possible values for the third side if two sides are 5 cm and 8 cm?
flashcards.a: The third side c must satisfy: 3 < c < 13 (i.e., |8-5| < c < 8+5).

flashcards.q: Can a triangle exist with sides 7, 7, and 14?
flashcards.a: No, because 7 + 7 = 14, which is not greater than 14.

flashcards.q: In triangle ABC, ∠A = ∠B. What type of triangle is it?
flashcards.a: Isosceles, with sides BC and AC equal (opposite the equal angles).

flashcards.q: Can a triangle have angles 45°, 45°, and 90°?
flashcards.a: Yes, this is an isosceles right-angled triangle.

flashcards.q: If all three sides of a triangle are equal, what are all three angles?
flashcards.a: All three angles are 60° each.

flashcards.q: Define the three types of triangles by sides.
flashcards.a: Equilateral (all equal), Isosceles (two equal), Scalene (all different).

flashcards.q: Define the three types of triangles by angles.
flashcards.a: Acute (all < 90°), Right (one = 90°), Obtuse (one > 90°).

flashcards.q: How many different triangles can be formed with sides 2 cm, 3 cm, 4 cm?
flashcards.a: Exactly one triangle, as the three sided lengths uniquely determine the triangle (SSS).

flashcards.q: What is a compass used for in triangle construction?
flashcards.a: To draw arcs of specific radii, locating points at fixed distances.

flashcards.q: In construction, what does 'bisect' mean?
flashcards.a: To divide into two equal parts.

flashcards.q: Can we construct a triangle with two sides 3 cm and 5 cm and any angle?
flashcards.a: Yes, but only certain angles allow the triangle to close properly (SAS method).

flashcards.q: What is the relationship between sides and angles in any triangle?
flashcards.a: The larger side is opposite the larger angle, and the smaller side is opposite the smaller angle.

flashcards.q: In a right triangle with angles 30°, 60°, and 90°, which side is longest?
flashcards.a: The side opposite the 90° angle (the hypotenuse).

flashcards.q: What is the basic property that makes triangle inequality work?
flashcards.a: The straight-line distance between two points is shorter than any other path connecting them.

flashcards.q: For circle-based SSS construction, why do we need two circles to intersect?
flashcards.a: The two circles (centered at endpoints of base with radii equal to the other two sides) must meet at a point; that point becomes the third vertex.

flashcards.q: When constructing SSS, if the circles don't intersect internally, what does it mean?
flashcards.a: The three side lengths do not satisfy the triangle inequality, so no triangle can exist.
